input.headline: New study finds that fluoride in drinking water may not affect cognitive ability

raw.headline: New study finds that fluoride in drinking water may not affect cognitive ability

winner: Fluoride may not dull your mind, but it sure does make your smile brighter—apparently, it’s the only thing in life that needs a warning label before you drink it.

candidates.text: I guess my brain still works just fine—must be all the fluoride in my coffee.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on fluoride source, concise and specific with a touch of self-deprecating humor."}

candidates.text: I always thought the fluoride in my water was making me less intelligent, but it turns out it’s actually just making me smart enough to realize I’m still not getting any work done.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on the study's finding, concise and specific with a relatable punchline."}

candidates.text: I guess my brain is still foggy from all those years of brushing my teeth — turns out, fluoride doesn't just make your teeth shine, it also keeps your thoughts from shining too bright.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on fluoride's effects, concise and specific with a clever play on 'shining'"}

candidates.text: I’m glad the study found that fluoride doesn't affect cognitive ability, because I was starting to worry that my inability to remember where I parked my car was a side effect of my tap water.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on a common concern, specific and concise with natural humor."}

candidates.text: Why did I start a fluoride-free diet? Because my IQ rose faster than my grocery bill!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.0, "reason": "The joke is concise, surprising, and ties directly to the headline with a clever twist involving IQ and grocery bills."}

candidates.text: Fluoride may not dull your mind, but it sure does make your smile brighter—apparently, it’s the only thing in life that needs a warning label before you drink it.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on fluoride's effects, concise and specific with a clever play on warning labels."}

candidates.text: Scientists are relieved that fluoride doesn't affect cognitive ability, because if it did, we'd have a much harder time finding the words to say it.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Smart wordplay on 'finding the words' ties into both science and language, with a surprising twist."}

candidates.text: I asked a tooth for its opinion on the study, and it said, "Well, if fluoride doesn't affect our cognitive ability, why does my morning coffee taste like a dentist’s regret?
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat creative with the personification of a tooth and the metaphor about coffee tasting like a dentist’s regret. However, it lacks clarity in connecting fluoride to the taste of coffee, which may confuse some readers. The humor is mild and not particularly surprising or specific."}

candidates.text: So maybe we should start adding lead to our toothpaste just to be safe?
